reasoning: The SSRF finding is confirmed real via full path trace. Entry gate at CEI_Core::init() requires current_user_can('edit_theme_options'), then _import() additionally checks can_import() which requires current_user_can('install_plugins') — matching the finding's claimed auth. A valid nonce is required. The attacker uploads a crafted .dat file; the file is @unserialize()'d with allowed_classes=>false (blocking PHP object injection but allowing arbitrary arrays). The deserialized $data['mods'] is attacker-controlled. A template name check (line 308) is a weak gate: the attacker can export first to learn the active theme, or guess common theme names. When the 'cei-import-images' request parameter is set, _import_images() iterates every value in $data['mods'] and calls _is_image_url() which only checks preg_match('/\.(jpg|jpeg|png|gif)/i', $string) — trivially satisfied by any URL containing a matching extension anywhere in the path (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/role.jpg). Strings passing that check go to _sideload_image(), where preg_match('/[^\?]+\.(jpe?g|jpe|gif|png)\b/i', $file, $matches) is used to extract a filename from the URL; this also passes for http://169.254.169.254/foo.jpg. Then download_url($file) is called at line 418 with the raw attacker-controlled URL string. There is no call to wp_http_validate_url(), no filter_var URL validation, no scheme allowlist, and no IP/host blocklist anywhere in the image import path. The WordPress download_url() function makes an HTTP GET request to the supplied URL, enabling SSRF against internal network addresses including cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254). No sanitizer or guard was missed.
